name: Affigere
classification_notes: The page presents Affigere as a display typeface with bold, poster-scale lettering evocative of French public notice typography and bill-posting tradition.
summary: A display typeface drawn from the visual language of French public notice typography, referencing the 1881 bill-posting law.
description: Affigere takes its cues from the large, blunt lettering found on French public notices and bill-posting culture, with the page invoking the familiar 'Défense d'afficher' prohibition. The design occupies the territory of vernacular display type suited to posters and large-scale graphic work. Little technical detail is available from the page, but the typographic character is bold and rooted in a specific cultural tradition.
foundry_blurb: X Cicéro presents Affigere with reference to the French law of 29 July 1881, evoking the classic 'Défense d'afficher' bill-posting prohibition and the graphic heritage of public notices.

name: Américaine
classification_notes: The page references Hamilton wood type from 1870s America and labels it 'Modern', pointing to a revival of 19th-century American wood type display letterforms typical of poster and headline printing.
summary: A revival of 19th-century American wood type in a single Roman weight, drawn from Hamilton wood type sources circa 1870.
description: Taking cues from Hamilton wood type of the 1870s, Américaine recalls the bold display letterforms used in American poster printing of the period. A single Roman weight is offered, keeping the family tightly focused on headline and display use. The labelling as 'Modern' on the specimen page situates it within the stylistic conventions of that era rather than the typographic classification of the same name.
foundry_blurb: X Cicéro present Américaine as a wood type revival referencing Hamilton's American display types of circa 1870, described on the specimen page as 'Modern' and shown in a single Roman weight with specimen text evoking period American graphic culture.

name: Antique Série
classification_notes: The name 'Antique Série' and the foundry's experimental ethos suggest a serif in the antique or early grotesque-adjacent tradition; the sparse page content prevents further classification precision.
summary: An experimentally framed serif from X Cicéro, the student type foundry of ESAD Grenoble-Valence, oriented around reduction and simplification.
description: Released under X Cicéro's philosophy of the 'dé-projet', a design methodology that removes rather than adds, Antique Série sits within a conceptual framework of stripping back information and material to its essentials. The foundry's programme at ESAD Grenoble-Valence positions the work as a critique of accumulation in design process. Little technical detail is available from the page, but the editorial framing suggests a typeface suited to considered, stripped-back typographic applications.
foundry_blurb: X Cicéro describes the 'dé-projet' as a project conceived in reverse: rather than increasing quantities of information and matter, it removes, reduces, mimics and simplifies them, rationalising mechanisms that have become jammed.

name: Biondeggiare
classification_notes: The page presents large, fragmented uppercase and lowercase text arranged compositionally rather than as running copy, suggesting a display typeface with expressive, experimental qualities suited to poster or editorial use.
summary: An experimental display typeface from X Cicéro whose page presents letterforms as fragmented, compositional arrangements rather than conventional specimens.
description: The specimen page for Biondeggiare uses a French-language text broken across lines in a deliberately compositional, almost typographic-art manner, giving a strong impression of a display typeface intended for expressive editorial or poster contexts. Minimal information is provided about weights or technical specifications. X Cicéro is the student type foundry associated with ESAD Grenoble-Valence, and Biondeggiare sits within that tradition of experimental, pedagogically driven type releases.
foundry_blurb: The page presents the typeface through a fragmented French-language phrase arranged across the screen in large uppercase and mixed-case type, offering an evocative specimen rather than a technical overview.

description: Bonbance is a display typeface released by X Cicéro, the type foundry associated with ESAD Grenoble-Valence. name: Caramel
classification_notes: The page presents no conventional type specimen or classification data; the content is a stylised recipe layout suggesting a display typeface used for decorative, expressive purposes.
summary: A display typeface from X Cicéro whose specimen takes the form of a caramel recipe, suggesting an expressive, food-themed design identity.
description: Released by X Cicéro, the student foundry of ESAD Grenoble-Valence, Caramel presents itself through a playful recipe layout rather than a conventional type specimen. The styling of the page implies a decorative display face suited to editorial and packaging contexts. Little technical detail is available, but the concept-led approach is consistent with the foundry's experimental output.
foundry_blurb: X Cicéro presents Caramel through a recipe for salted-butter caramel sauce, listing 10cl of full-fat cream, 50g of salted butter, and 100g of sugar, letting the typographic treatment of the recipe serve as the specimen itself.

name: Cesare
classification_notes: The letterforms shown are large-scale, highly stylised serif characters intended for headline or display use, with no indication of text-weight variants.
summary: A display serif from the X Cicéro student foundry, built for large-scale headline use with a bold, graphic character.
description: Cesare presents a display serif with strong contrasts and a graphic sensibility suited to poster and editorial applications. The available specimen shows the typeface at large sizes across mixed-case settings, suggesting a focus on visual impact over extended text use. foundry_blurb: Cesare is a display typeface published through X Cicéro, the type foundry associated with ESAD Grenoble-Valence.

name: Excessive
classification_notes: The automotive and speed-related naming of weights suggests a display typeface with expressive, high-energy characteristics suited to headlines and posters rather than text setting.
summary: A display typeface with six idiosyncratically named weights drawn from an automotive vocabulary, suggesting high-energy use in headlines and poster work.
description: Six weights named after automotive references — from Automobile through to Free Gasoline — give this display face a distinctly irreverent character. The naming convention implies a progression from lighter to heavier cuts, framed around speed and movement. Suited to editorial headlines, posters and identity work where a bold, unconventional voice is needed.
foundry_blurb: Excessive is a display typeface from X Cicéro featuring six weights with automotive-themed names: Automobile, Motorcycles, Backseat, Speedometer, Expressway and Free Gasoline.

name: Egyptienne Large
classification_notes: The name 'Egyptienne' is the French term for Egyptian or slab serif typefaces; the large-scale display specimens suggest a display-oriented slab serif.
summary: A large-display slab serif from X Cicéro, the type foundry associated with ESAD Grenoble-Valence, taking its name from the French typographic tradition of Egyptienne classification.
description: Egyptienne Large draws on the French naming convention for slab serif typefaces and is pitched at display and poster scales. The specimen text — short, punchy French phrases set in heavy capitals — suggests a typeface built for large, assertive settings. Released through X Cicéro, the student and research foundry of ESAD Grenoble-Valence.
foundry_blurb: The page presents Egyptienne Large through a series of short French phrases set at display size, foregrounding its use in bold, large-scale typographic contexts.

name: Egyptienne E
classification_notes: The typeface name and visual specimen suggest a slab serif in the Egyptian tradition, characterised by heavy bracketed or unbracketed serifs of uniform stroke weight typical of 19th-century Egyptian display types.
summary: A display slab serif in the Egyptian tradition, shown in bold headline settings with a direct, declarative character.
description: Egyptienne E draws on the Egyptian slab serif tradition, with the specimen text suggesting a use case rooted in large-scale display and poster work. Little technical detail is available, but the typeface reads as a focused single-weight display cut from the X Cicéro student foundry at ESAD Grenoble-Valence.
foundry_blurb: The X Cicéro page for Egyptienne E presents the typeface through a series of bold declarative French phrases, implying a font designed for display use with a strong, assertive visual presence.

name: Rosette
classification_notes: The page specimen uses large, characterful letterforms set across Lyonnaise culinary terms and phrases, suggesting a display serif with regional typographic flavour. The name and presentation point to a decorative, display-oriented classification.
summary: A display typeface from X Cicéro shown entirely through a specimen of classic Lyonnaise dishes and culinary culture.

The approach ties the typeface's identity to a strong regional vernacular. Beyond the specimen content, technical details such as weights, italics and language coverage are not disclosed on the page.
foundry_blurb: X Cicéro present Rosette through a specimen steeped in the food culture of Lyon, running the typeface across traditional dishes and local culinary references to demonstrate its character.

name: Thestral
classification_notes: The fragmentary page content and visual language — matrices, colour filters, negative strips — suggest an experimental display typeface drawing on phototypesetting or analogue reproduction processes.
summary: An experimental display typeface from X Cicéro referencing 1930s photomechanical and colour-separation processes.
description: The page situates Thestral within a visual vocabulary of matrices, three-strip negatives and colour filters, evoking the technical language of phototypesetting and early colour reproduction. The 1930 date reference frames the design within a period of significant mechanical printing experimentation. Little further detail is publicly available, which is consistent with X Cicéro's position as a student or experimental foundry platform.
foundry_blurb: X Cicéro presents Thestral through fragmented references to 1930s processes: matrices, three-strip negative colour filters, and analogue reproduction — suggesting a typeface conceived around the aesthetics of early photomechanical printing.
